then.verdict: Retake #3 is a low-odds, high-cost bet
then.headline: Another CAT attempt at 5–10 prep hours/week is statistically a long shot for A/B/C calls
why: IIM A/B/C shortlists for General-category engineer males have typically required ~99.9th percentile — each IIM publishes its admission policy; check the current year's PDF.
Moving from the 95–98 band to 99.9+ is a step-change in preparation, not a polish — it rarely happens on unchanged weekly hours.
Each retake costs a year of salary growth, promotion track, and compounding — and the same season could fund a GMAT/GRE cycle that keeps global doors open.
pros: If it lands, IIM A/B/C at ₹25–35L total cost is close to unbeatable ROI
cons: Category + academic-background cutoff math is working against you and won't change

worst_case.scenario: A third score in the 96–98 band, no A/B/C call, and one more year gone
worst_case.mitigation: Run GMAT prep in parallel — large syllabus overlap; a strong GMAT the same season keeps ISB and global MBAs open

actions.label: See your GMAT/GRE fork
actions.how: Pathways → exam fork, your profile applied

then.headline: CAT, GMAT and GRE are not interchangeable — they open different rooms at different prices
why: CAT: India-only door. Cheapest tuition on the other side (IIM ₹25–35L all-in), but one shot per year, score valid one season, and shortlist cutoffs depend on your category-gender-background pool.
GMAT: the ISB + global MBA door. Retakeable (5 attempts per rolling year, 8 lifetime), score valid 5 years — one strong season keeps options open across countries and years.
GRE: the MS/MEM door with growing MBA acceptance. Cheapest optionality per rupee: one exam covers engineering-management, analytics, CS and a widening set of MBA programs.
pros: Your budget and loan access, not the exam, should pick the door — the matrix below is personalized to both
GMAT/GRE prep overlaps heavily with CAT quant/verbal — a parallel plan wastes little
cons: Chasing all three at once usually means clearing none at the level that matters
GMAT/GRE-route degrees abroad cost 2–5× an IIM — the door is global, the price is too

worst_case.scenario: A season spent split across exams, with no score strong enough for any target list
worst_case.mitigation: Pick ONE primary exam for this season from the matrix's 'choose this if' lines; keep the second as a planned next season, not a parallel one

then.verdict: Rank countries by PR predictability, not brand
then.headline: If paperwork tolerance is minimal, pick the queue you can see the end of
why: Canada Express Entry is points-math you can compute today — age, education, language, experience — and the pathway is the same for everyone in the pool.
Australia's points system is similar: transparent, capricious only at the margins.
Germany: Blue Card → permanent residence in 21–27 months (with language milestones) is one of the shortest credible PR clocks anywhere.
UK: Graduate Route gives 2 years post-study without sponsorship; the Skilled Worker → settlement path is rule-based.
Singapore EP is employer-driven and PR is discretionary — genuinely great to live in, but you cannot compute your odds. Say that honestly.
US: for Indian-born applicants, the EB2/EB3 employment green-card backlog is measured in DECADES absent EB1/NIW routes. Check the live Visa Bulletin — never trust a stored date.
pros: Points systems reward exactly the profile you can build deliberately: language scores, degrees, years
cons: Predictable queues are also popular queues — cutoffs drift up
Optimizing for PR speed can cost salary ceiling vs the US

worst_case.scenario: You optimize for the US on 'minimal hassle' tolerance and spend 15+ years on visa renewals, stamping trips, and I-140 anxiety
worst_case.mitigation: Either recalibrate tolerance honestly, or pick a points country while keeping US employment via multinational transfers open

actions.label: Check the live Visa Bulletin
